content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K Street
Sacramento 95814                        January 26, 1976
(916) 322-5660


     Edwin C. Bliss
     National Association of Manufacturers
     P.O. Box 998
     Mountain View, CA. 94042

     Dear Mr. Bliss:

          Thank you for your letter of December 11, 1975, concern-
     ing your obligations under the Political Reform Act. Your
     letter indicates that your organization intends to limit its
     political activities to involvement with candidates for federal
     office. If your organization follows this course, it will be
     outside the scope of the California law with one possible
     exception.

          California Government Code Section 84208, the only pro-
     vision of the Political Reform Act which pertains to federal
     campaigns, provides:

          Every person who is required by Section 309(a)
          of the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 (2
          U.S.C.A. Section 439(a)) to file a copy of any statement
          or report with the Secretary of State of California
          shall, at the time such filing is required, file two
          copies of each statement or report with the Secretary
          of State, one of which shall have an original signa-
          ture, and two copies with:
               (a) The clerk of Los Angeles County and the Clerk
               of the City and County of San Francisco in the case
               of reports relating to a campaign for nomination or
               election of a candidate to the office of President or
               Vice-President of the United States, or United States
               Senator;
               (b) The clerk of each county in which the congres-
               sional district is located in the case of reports re-
               lating to the campaign for nomination or election of
               a candidate to the office of Representative in Congress.

          However, the effect of this section is unclear at the present
     time.

          After passage of the Political Reform Act, the Federal
     Elections Campaign Act was amended and, included in the amend-
     ments, was the following provision:

          The provisions of this Act, and of rules prescribed
          under this Act, supersede and preempt any provision
          of State law with respect to election to federal office.

                                   2 U.S.C. §453

Mr. Edwin C. Bliss
January 26, 1976
Page Two


Since it is unclear whether it was intended that 2 U.S.C.
Section 453 would supersede and preempt all state laws includ-
ing those which, like Section 84208, are essentially procedural,
the Fair Political Practices Commission has asked the Federal
Election Commission for an opinion concerning the correct inter-
pretation of the federal provision.

The opinion request has been pending since November, 1975,
and, in all candor, I expect the Federal Election Commission to
conclude that Section 84208 is superseded. However, pending
receipt of the opinion, I cannot be certain and, to be safe,
you may wish to comply with the California provision.

I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any further
questions, please contact Ted Prim, an attorney with the Com-
mission staff.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

DHL:pvp

NATIONAL ASSOCIATION OF MANUFACTURERS

Edwin C. Bliss
Public Affairs Director

December 11, 1975

Mr. Dan Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1015 L Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

I have been asked whether Proposition 9 would affect
in any way an organization set up to obtain campaign
funds for candidates to the United States Congress.
My understanding is that it would not, but I would
appreciate your confirming this in writing, since I
am not intimately familiar with the state law on such
matters.

In addition, it would be appreciated if you would send
me a copy of the guidelines which you have prepared,
and a copy of the text of Proposition 9 if that is
available.

Your cooperation is appreciated.

Sincerely,

Edwin C. Bliss

ECB/at

P. O. Box 998, Mountain View, California 94042 • Phone (415) 965-4242 • TWX: 910-379-6554

sections.question:
s, please contact Ted Prim, an attorney with the Com-
mission staff. 
DHL:pvp
sections.conclusion: If your organization follows this course [limiting activities to federal candidates], it will be outside the scope of the California law with one possible exception. [The FPPC discusses California Government Code Section 84208 as the only relevant provision, but notes that the Federal Elections Campaign Act amendments may supersede this state law. The FPPC has requested an opinion from the Federal Election Commission and expects Section 84208 to be superseded, but recommends compliance with California provisions pending that determination.]
sections.question_synthetic: Does Proposition 9 (California's Political Reform Act) apply to organizations that limit their political activities to obtaining campaign funds for federal candidates for Congress?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Organizations limiting activities to federal candidates are generally outside California's Political Reform Act scope, with the possible exception of California Government Code Section 84208's filing requirements. However, due to potential federal preemption issues, the FPPC recommends complying with California provisions pending a Federal Election Commission interpretation.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (December 11, 1975) and the FPPC response letter (January 26, 1976). Some OCR errors present (e.g., 'Cowilllission' for 'Commission', 'V.S.C.' for 'U.S.C.'). FPPC response discusses pending Federal Election Commission opinion on preemption, creating conditional/uncertain advice.

mission staff.

CONCLUSION: If your organization follows this course [limiting activities to federal candidates], it will be outside the scope of the California law with one possible exception. [The FPPC discusses California Government Code Section 84208 as the only relevant provision, but notes that the Federal Elections Campaign Act amendments may supersede this state law. The FPPC has requested an opinion from the Federal Election Commission and expects Section 84208 to be superseded, but recommends compliance with California provisions pending that determination.]

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Edwin C. Bliss of the National Association of Manufacturers that an organization limiting its political activities to federal candidates for Congress would generally fall outside California's Political Reform Act, except for possible filing requirements under Section 84208, though federal preemption may ultimately render that provision inapplicable.
